metadata.title: AMA: Inside the OWASP State of Agentic AI Security & Governance

segments.text: Hello, good morning, good evening
everyone. We welcome you here with us.
Thank you so much for joining us today.
I'm Karen Katz. I'm Zenity director for
I security and OASPLET for the Agentic
top 10 and I'm really happy to be here
with my friends today to talk about the
new release of the state of Agentic AI
document. Uh I think we'll start with
the introductions. Rock.
>> Sure. Thanks Karen. Hey everyone. I'm
Rock Lamros. Um I had the honor uh and
pleasure of co-leading this report
alongside Ariel and Nimi here. My
day-to-day role is the director of AI
standards and governance at Zenity and
I'm also on the core team of the OASP
Genai security project um agentic
security initiative. I've been in the
industry about 30 years and I've run
global socks and I'm also recovering
critical infrastructure CISO which is a
fancy way of saying really that I've
been the person who answers for it when
the thing that nobody worries about or
cares or uh cares about breaks. And I
think that's the lens that I bring to
this conversation and to this report.
Not that like, hey, AI is this new and
exciting thing, but more like who's
holding that pager when the agent does
something really stupid at 3:00 a.m.
>> Thanks, Rock. Ariel, next to you.
>> Yeah. Hi. So, my name is Ariel. I am the
uh founding engineer and also an AI
security researcher now uh at the office
of the CTO at Pillar Security. That's
quite a mouthful. Um but I I've been um
focusing on agentic risks, AI risks for
the last uh three plus years now. And um
yeah, I've been getting increasingly
involved uh in this initiative. There's
another initiative um in terms of like
uh agent control standards that I'm on
the core team of and yeah, aside from
that uh excited to be here.
>> Good to have you with us. And last but
not least, if Gani.
>> Hi everyone. Uh I'm very excited to join
today's webinar. Uh my name is Genei. I
run AI security research at itmo
university and I run a company called
Hive Trace. And uh I've been into AI
security since the first jailbreak. We
saw how it appeared. And uh over the
last couple of years I joined OASP
various OASP initiatives. And I'm I'm
very excited today uh we talk about the
state of AI security and governance
report the second version. So the
amazing team many contributors uh was
involved uh to making this report go
live.
>> Sounds so good. It's really good to talk
with all of you because I remember the
first report of the state of Agent AI
and we're smiling because we had lots of
discussions around the name of the
report because I remember that our
intention was to create a report that
will really lay the foundation to all
the other work that OASP is doing
because it's really hard to just jump
into a Gentai without having any
foundations and with these foundations
change over time, it's really hard to
keep up. So my first question to you
would be why second version now? Why to
have another document? Why do you have
this update right now?
>> Yeah, I'll take that one. So I mean I
think we could all agree that the
landscape is moving crazy fast, right?
So last year, I think it was Julyish
when we dropped the first version of the
report. That was more of a forecast, if
you will, it was a bunch of smart people
saying, "Here's what we think will go
wrong. Um, and when we think these
things are going to get real." I mean,
it was really good work, but we didn't
have a lot of data um or real incidents
behind it. So now a year later, we don't
have to forecast, right? We can point at
things. Almost every category that we
forecasted last year now has a CVE
assigned to it, a vendor advisory, a
real campaign having a real bad day,
right? Or a real company having a really
bad day. And you know, we've seen a
coding agent wiping production databases
after being told repeatedly not to touch
anything. We have emails that have
walked out confidential data out of a
co-pilot with zero clicks. Right? So
what I want to emphasize is the 2026
version of the report. It's not just uh
fresh paint over last year's version.
It's a total rebuild, right? We rebuilt
a thread analysis around documented
cases. We added a section on why safety
and security stop being separate once
the agent acts. We added a maturity
model so you could kind of find where
you stand with regards to your agentic
enterprise deployments. Right? the the
ground shifted beneath us in a wild way
over the last 12 months and I think more
than anything that's the story here.
I totally get it and I think that I do
remember when we worked on the first
version of the document that we were
looking for incidents that's been
published and we couldn't find this like
no incident been published back then
like there were a few but there weren't
real incidents and we had data about
incidents because all of us working in
AI security companies obviously were
seeing the incidents daytoday but we're
we weren't allowed to talk about them
and I think a major shift happened since
then is that we now see that some of
these incidents are being published
which is really critical for our uh
community and you know the first part of
this document is the agenda tonomy. I
remember that we defined it. I remember
writing it and thinking about coining
the terms and a year later I can share
that almost any client or prospect that
I'm talking with actually use this terms
which is pretty pretty much exciting and
I wonder what are the key changes in
this document document in terms of
taxonomy and why did we made them?
>> Yeah. Um I'd be happy to take that. So
um I actually ended up spending a lot of
time on the agent taxonomy uh on this
time around and I think one of the big
things that we've noticed like again
we've all kind of been suffering from a
sense of whiplash with how rapidly
things have changed. And so while the
taxonomy that was defined might have
been really appropriate for what was
happening a year ago at this point we've
just found that there was so many
different layers and dimensions and
wanted to make this as useful as
possible for specifically uh CISOs or uh
security executives to be able to make
useful decisions that help them evaluate
the risk of what kind of um AI was
deployed in their organiz organization
and so for that purposes we ended up
breaking this down into a few different
uh axes. Um the first one was focused on
agent types. Um and then we talked about
how those agent types so those are more
focused on kind of like the the domains.
So what the agent does, where it
operates, um things like are they
enterprise facing uh coding, client
facing, some of these will feel familiar
from the previous uh taxonomy, but then
we talked about what are the ways in
which they're built, right? Are they
using particular uh frameworks or are
they no code, low code? Um and and how
are they actually composed, right? Is it
a single agent with tools? Is it a
multi- aent system? Those are all kind
of themes that that kind of got a little
bit of an update. But in in composing
this taxonomy, we also noticed that one
thing that was crosscutting across all
of these different domains was the
autonomy level. And this is really
important when you're trying to evaluate
risk because the the less autonomy it
has if it's supervised. So an a human
might approve every single agent action.
Uh so the agent suggests something and
the human will execute it or if it's
semi-autonomous. This is a little bit
riskier, right? Um but the the real risk
happens when you have the the fully
autonomous agents where you just let
them go. And that's really kind of one
of the the key ideas that we were trying
to say is that uh this level of autonomy
um this is crosscutting across all of
these domains.
Thanks. I think I think that's a really
good reason to to actually update the
taxonomy and and I wonder what is your
advice for readers in matters of what
should they take from this texonomy? How
do they take that into real life
environment into their enterprises? What
should they learn from it?
I mean, I think that that one of the the
core ideas is that um when you're trying
to evaluate what exactly are the risks
associated with the agents that you have
deployed in your organization, part of
it is going to be what what are the
domains that it's working in? Is it
talking to is it dealing with, you know,
sensitive data in terms of like a coding
agent? It might have more access to
credentials versus something that's
client facing. Um so this is one axis
where you can kind of to evaluate risk.
The other thing is how is it supported?
You know did you roll your own framework
or did you use a no code low code how is
that overseen and what are the vendor
evaluations for you know trusting
trusting that framework. Um I think
[snorts] the the other thing is you know
as as was mentioned how does it compose?
So is it just one agent for with pools
right that's going to maybe have a
different blast radius relative to a
multi- aent system where one can spawn
another or can hand off to another and
again the cross cutting thing and this
is really helping to evaluate that blast
radius is is uh the level of autonomy.
So really by by decomposing your agents
on a case- by case basis to understand
what exactly are the components that
make it up, that's how you'll be able to
start to understand and evaluate whether
or not there's an existing risk that you
need to address today versus something
that you can kind of kick off a little
bit later.
>> And you know, I think it's it's very
interesting. Yes, if go on.
>> Yes, I'd like to add a specific comment
to to your question, Karen. So uh given
the uh constraints we we have on the
length and the the document is already
more than 100 pages long uh we cannot
provide uh fully details for each
particular agent type uh how how uh how
to protect and what are the particular
risk but for example even the popular
personal type of agents we have a
dedicated appendix in the document. So
uh the the reason why I want to
highlight because this is the last
appendix in a document probably not all
of the readers will put an attention but
if you are facing this challenges
securing this type of u personal agent
such as open claw you can find more
details how it aligns to the vaspogentic
top 10 in in regards to specific risks.
Okay back to you Karen.
Yeah, I think that's that's a good
highlight. And also I think that Ariel
mentioned that it's it will be
beneficial for companies to actually
evaluate and map their agents
considering the different categories
mapped in the taxonomy. And what I think
is that I want to take this to the next
level because obviously when companies
has thousands of agents if not like
hundreds of thousands, it's really hard
to do it one by one. But what I wouldn't
think of is to try and understand how
the system that you're using, the
monitoring and the response capabilities
that you're defining actually maps to
these families of categories instead of
just like mapping them one by one
because I totally agree that that would
be ideal. But maybe like in a word when
we have so many agents in your
applications embedded in your
applications and in your operations, it
would be very hard. But another
implementation could be like validate
how can you make sure that properties
that might uh infer on which agent it is
are actually visible uh to the
visibility layer that you're applying
all over your agents and then like if
you investigate or if you're applying
any policies you can actually use this
taxonomy to manage your agents in real
life. just some thoughts and you know
this report actually look at key trends
of the industry and what is the new
direction and where we're heading and
I'm curious to hear from you what are
the key trends that you identified in
there
>> I'd like to uh to take this question so
uh as readers may already noticed the
report has lots of uh sections inside
but uh uh personally for me some section
uh sections outlined for example this
year we specifically wanted to touch on
a on the safety point uh when we
released the first version uh we had a
quite a discussion whether we should
include uh some points on not only on
security but on safety so this year we
deliberately uh added the content uh
there uh and I think we will probably uh
get back to to this uh as well uh also
one of The uh
challenging topic is uh non-human
identities and a agent identities which
are not the same thing and we have a uh
few sections uh we have a dedicated
section in the report regarding the
topic and also mentioned in a threat
modeling and f future trends how uh this
problem might be more more important in
the next few months and uh obviously uh
since the last version we have uh good
coverage on regulatory landscape and uh
I think uh Rock will share more about
this one but what we can clearly see
some of the agentic deployments are uh
far away from the current uh regulatory
requirements and it's and it's hard to
comply and u we have this small section
on
um explainable AI so many regulatory
documents they require for us to give
some understanding how model works, how
genic system works. But uh we point out
it's not easy to implement due to the
technical uh challenges uh in the
systems.
Rock, would you would you like to uh uh
to add uh on uh your uh from your point
on important trends?
>> I mean, I think you nailed them all,
right? Like I think for me there's
almost a a counter trend in there
um where
if you look at the advisories you could
kind of track adoption, right? Um if you
look at the top five CVES I think that
we tracked, they're all kind of
semi-autonomous frameworks, not the
scary
fully autonomous stuff, right? So I
would say like that's an indication that
when you're prioritizing risks
uh with regards to your agents, you
know, often times people immediately go
to well what's fully autonomous and
that's not necessarily the case, right?
Based on on the data that that we're
seeing, right? So it should really
prioritize by footprint and not by like
how autonomous the agent is.
And [clears throat] al also I'd like to
uh to mention two specific sections. So
we did a uh a small survey for the
popular open source project. Uh and this
is a uh uh site deliverables of of this
report. So from that s uh survey we
analyze the uh most popular uh GitHub
repositories and and see the uh the data
points. So in the report you can find
the uh this visuals and for example for
what surprised me the most uh some of
the most popular projects have thousands
of pull requests and open issues and
even more surprisingly uh even with
small number of maintainers with
advanced AI tools they able to close
like 80% of open issues uh efficiently
u and um another one uh is uh incident
tracker this um a ded a separate OASP uh
agentic initiative project. It will be
released separately but in the report
you may you will find the preview
showing the listing all the important
incidents split by the SI categories.
>> So if you're bringing you bring me up to
the next question which was the threat
analysis. So we already said that last
year we tried to look at the threats and
we tried to look at incidents and to
find some that we can share and it was
really hard. So I want to hear more
about how what was it like this year?
>> Yeah, I'll take that one. Um
I think the interesting thing this year
compared to last year is that the
fundamental categories
that things got bucketed into barely
changed. But what did change is the
effectively the status between each one.
Right? Whereas last year we may have had
as I mentioned like earlier more
forecasting or speculative this year we
actually have like documented incidents
with the CVE. And I think the big shift
and this is where I'll actually plant a
flag is like that we see like we see
this in the report and we see this in a
lot of the research um we're doing right
now for the LLM top 10 update that I'm
co-leading with Steve Wilson is everyone
calls prompt ejection the vulnerability
and it's not like what we're seeing it's
the delivery mechanism. It's the
delivery truck with the payload uh that
rides inside of it and the damage maps
to the categories we already had which
is why you'll see injection showing up
in kind of six out of the 10 categories
in the report. So treating it as a
checkbox that you can solve like you're
you're just going to waste time but
instead like figure out how to contain
the blast radius around it. And I think
the other big one um in the threat
analysis that came up was supply chain,
right? Last year was kind of a footnote
this year. I mean it's by like by far
probably the fastest moving attack
surface, right? Like attackers stopped
attacking the agent and started
positioning
what they or started um poisoning rather
like what the agent trusts, right? So
tool descriptions, memory file,
dependencies,
the agent's own capability became the
weapon.
>> [snorts]
>> Yeah, I think um just piggybacking off
of that, I certainly internally we've
been talking about um
about uh prompt injection as a TTP and
and one thing that I think is really
fascinating is just how much first of
all because of agentic adoption uh it
has just created a huge target not just
on the agents themselves but also on the
infrastructure that supports them. Um
and so we've seen these crazy campaigns
that have created uh really in strong
lateral movement between uh projects
that are critical AI infrastructure that
then create uh downstream effects and
they're still um having ripple effects
today. Um, but aside from that, just
kind of going back to the the TTP
perspective um, of prompt injection that
supply chain, we also saw so many
different novel things that had changed
in terms of how agents receive those
instructions that can be hijacked and
then the potential blast radius
associated with that. So whether it be
uh, MCP uh, new threat models associated
with MCPS, whether it be skill
registrations um, skill registries
rather and and how skills can be
malicious themselves. Um these are all
kind of new marketplaces, plugins as
well for coding agents. Um, these are
all new supply chains that are being
quickly adopted and are not necessarily
being scrutinized well um or or
understood how they're connected to
which agents and the what the potential
blast radius could be at any given
moment.
>> You know, it's bring me it brings me to
another point that I remember that last
year when you worked on the agentic
protocol uh landscape part. So Josh
really helped us and it was in the very
first days of MCPS and A2A and it was
only the beginning of it and it's really
nice to to look back at it right now
because if Genny if you remember and
Rock if you remember how it felt very
new and we were curious about the
adoption of it and how it will look like
and I think it's been a year and it's
already everywhere. So how did the
process of refining and updating this
part of the report look like?
Uh I I can take this this one and uh and
definitely I remember in the first
version uh still there is a even the
description what is the MCP the the
short architectural schema and now uh
this is uh widely adopted uh standard
for connecting the agents to tools and
uh as regards to other protocols uh we
don't see this uh this high adoption as
MC CP and we highlighted that specific
in a report. Even A2A uh we often hear
A2A but uh uh we don't see uh much of
the A2A systems uh uh being publicly
discussed. Um so uh we put uh a notice
for for the reader uh for the overview
of the topic and in regards to MCP uh it
might be useful uh uh to look in more
details for the specific of MCP cheat
sheet uh being referenced in in in our
report and also given all all the guides
uh advisers and uh top 10 uh of us
producers uh we specifically for the
state of AI report. We maintain a
section called fit with other OASP
resources uh where you can find some
guidance and navigation between the
different documents because more than uh
30 documents were released over the last
year I think. Uh so we try to help to
navigate through this information. U
sorry Karen I I went a bit sidetracked
but uh
>> no I It's great.
>> I think the the hilarious part about
last year since you brought it up and
the thing that stood out to me the most
was when we started working on that
report,
MCP were just random letters in the
alphabet. We had to like scramble
towards like right before we released
the report to put something in there
about MCP, right? Because that's how
quickly the landscape moved. That's how
quickly it dropped. Anthropic dropped
it. That's how quickly it was picked up
by the Linux Foundation. and then we're
like, "Oh, this is a thing that we need
to include because we can't wait another
year to include it." Um, and
fortunately, we didn't have that kind of
scramble this year. But it just goes to
show like just how fast everything is
moving.
>> And the most notable
>> Oh, sorry.
>> Sorry. Come on.
Uh the most notable incident on MCP is
we widely reference is the postmark MCP
when uh this server was legitimate uh
for many releases and many people
dependent on that one and uh at some
point the new version came with a only
one uh uh only one line added u blind
copy to the uh attacker email. It it
shows the important of uh these threats.
That's why we we highlight and uh for
the next version likely to update this
as well.
>> Shout out to that actually published
this [clears throat] uh vulnerability.
It's one of my favorites and it leads me
to talk about AIS bomb that's been added
to the document as well which I think is
super important as as we are saying that
the adoption of of these supply chain
tools of MCPS and and connected a agents
have been wild now. So I think it is
makes a lot of sense to add this
component to the document and I wanted
to hear more about how you did it and
how it compare with the initiative that
we have in OASP about asbomb. So wonder
about wonder what your thoughts about
it.
>> Yeah I can take this. So this is all
about calling our friend Helen Oakley
who is leading the Ogen AI security
project AI sbomb AI bomb initiative
right so um so it's clearly very aligned
because she authored that part of the of
the report um like we know sbombs have
been a good idea for years right know
your dependencies on your artifacts AI
bombs extends it to model versions and
lineage right you need that baseline you
need to go do
um
OASP and and Helen's initiative publish
a tool that'll actually allow you to go
point to a model in hugging face, you
know, kind of give you a a score on on
it's AI bomb from a risk perspective.
Now, here's why I think agents kind of
force the next step, right? Traditional
ESBOM is a buildtime list. Agents, I
mean, we know they don't work that way,
right? They discover tools, they load
skills, they spawn sub aents at runtime.
The dependency graph assembles itself
live with every execution. So I think
your buildtime inventory describes a
system that stopped existing kind of the
moment that the agent ran. So the shift
I think in our mindset has to be from
the old question of what components are
present to the new question what can get
assembled at runtime and under whose
authority and for any highstakes
workflows you need to reconstruct
afterwards which tool and which agents
touch the outcome right no regulator
right now is calling it an AI bomb or an
AIS bomb or whatever you want to call it
but I think everyone everyone of them is
going to start asking for it much like
they're starting to ask for sbombs.
I think that it's really interesting to
think about it for a moment because
we're all all here calling for like
maintaining asbomb, reviewing the
versions, making sure there's like a
very specific process of how we update a
new version. But this all is nice in
theory, but in practice when I meet
companies and enterprises that actually
have MCPS in production, the birth truth
is that they don't even know what MCPS
are in use. So I think what we're saying
now is that this is the ideal situation.
We want to push you to do this. But even
starting from the very basics of
understanding why MCPs are in use. Um
who are who are the agents that using
them and then you know walking this
trail of finding out what the MCPs how
we maintain the versions what kill
switch do we want to have for malicious
MCPS how we monitor malicious MCPS. So
it's okay that it's a process but please
just start. Ariel you wanted to say
something. I did. Um, so I wanted to
just follow up on this, which is that
uh, one thing that that that you and
Rock have both said is that ESBOMs right
now are still relatively immature. Um,
AIS bombs are relatively immature in
that you don't have regulators quite
asking for them in exactly the way that
that uh, you know, much more mature
software uh, is asked for and you don't
have the same attestation process. Um
again I think that that not only is it
uh useful for us to to be moving towards
this but um there is a certain reckoning
within the folks uh who are responsible
for for even constructing these AIS
bombs for the fact that the language
that they have the actual like standards
be it cyclone DX or SPDX don't quite
reflect the the needs
of
of um how you can describe an agentic
system today. And so what you're seeing
right now actively and this is alluded
to in the report is a modification from
just describing the components to
actually describing the behavior of
these systems. the the grammar that
allows you to describe how a behav
um in production and that will help us
be much more kind of concrete so that we
don't get as surprised that even if all
of the components look safe when you
actually deploy to production at runtime
something completely different happens.
>> [snorts]
>> So you mentioned here a few times the
regulators and we got the regulation
king here with us rock and there is the
regulation party in the document. So it
all bring me to the question can we ask
for a brief of what been updated since
last year till this year on the
regulation front?
Oh boy.
[laughter]
I mean, the regulation landscape's kind
of a mess, right? And that's I'm I'm not
like dropping any secret nuggets there.
All you have to do is
open up any news article any any random
day and you'll see that. But I think
what
um this past year demonstrated is it is
like this is year that AI governance
actually got some legitimate like teeth
to it like some penalties attached to it
and although like not much has been
adjudicated yet but we mapped 42
instruments against 10 jurisdictions. Uh
in the US alone there's something like
175 different AI related regulations. So
there's no way we could have gotten into
everything and we definitely wanted a
global focus.
So I think the the reframing for this
year is the incident clocks. That's
really the the most interesting part,
right? Dora has a a 4-hour reporting
notification from when you confirm an
incident. Uh NIS2 wants a 24-hour early
warning. So if you even suspect an
incident, uh New York rays 72 hours.
California 15 days, right? So, but like
everyone assumes though like all of
these assume that continuous oversight
um
and
you know that people have the capability
to do continuous oversight on these
agents, right? No regulator actually
wrote the words do drift detection, but
how are you going to start your 4hour
clock on something that makes automated
decisions?
um
you know and then leveraging uh a
deterministic detection mechanism it's
going to be really difficult and then
the the US mess right like I mean it
like so we have an executive order that
preempts state laws and I mentioned like
there 140 I think I I might have
mentioned 175 there 145 uh plus state AI
laws passed in the past year all of them
with conflicting definitions Right?
There's no safe harbor yet.
So, plan for the strictest timeline that
touches you. Right? If your organization
is sitting around saying, "We're waiting
for clarity or we're waiting to see how
it all shakes out, like you're going to
end up, you could potentially end up in
a court of law." And that's not going to
hold up as a defense, right? So if I
were to try and rank kind of my worry
list if you will of the regulations and
the things that we need to pay attention
to would be first the EU AI act. That
hasn't changed in the past year, right?
It's still kind of the global standard.
Uh high-risk obligations are going to be
enforceable in August 26 for now, right?
The the proposed postponement to 2027
isn't official yet. What you really need
to pay attention there as security
leaders and practitioners is article 72.
It quietly demands drift detection
without using the words, right? Um so
like I said, don't bet your road map on
that delay, right? On the proposed
delay, like get on top of it now.
Then I mentioned the incident of clocks,
right, with Doran 4 hours and others.
And then the US executive orders like we
have the preeemption order. We have the
executive order that dropped last week
with regards to uh leveraging AI within
the US federal government and uh defense
industrial base. We have the CISA
binding oper operations directive that
dropped yesterday, day before yesterday
around categorizing and prioritizing
vulnerabilities with AI.
Um,
so the that landscape is moving fast and
I think the real thing that we need to
also pay attention to is that [snorts]
EU liability is shifting to a strict no
fault product liability effective at the
end of the year in December. So the
whole value chain, the whole supply
chain now could become a potential
defendant. And uh for those of you who
aren't like legal connoisseurs, no fault
product liability really just means if a
product hurts someone because it's
defective, the injured person doesn't
have to prove that the company was
careless, only that the product was
defective and caused harm, right? So
like the company doesn't even have to be
like at fault or have done anything
negligent. And so it's going to be
interesting to see what lawsuits and
